data.title: Aon: Almost Time To Take Out Insurance
data.description:
Shares of Aon (NYSE:AON) have been stagnating a bit in recent years despite the continuation of organic growth and M&A driven growth, which makes it so that valuation multiples over time have come down.

This makes me more upbeat from a risk-reward perspective, and amidst a very small premium compared to the market, I am lured to get involved here, although I would like to see a continued dip before pulling the trigger.

Powerful Platform For Growth

AON serves large, attractive, and growing markets, these being combined a more than $4.5 trillion health, commercial risk, and reinsurance market. The company brokers and provides risk management solutions for private equity, sovereign wealth funds, pension funds, and other clients.

The company benefits from strong growth from various trends, including larger losses from natural disasters amidst great occurrence and higher replacement costs, rising healthcare costs, increasing benefits as a percentage of compensation, and growing uncertainty about retirement among consumers.

The company generates about $16 billion in annual sales, of which half are generated from commercial risks, and the remainder from notable health, reinsurance, and wealth solutions. The company is very globally diversified, with half of sales generated in North America, nearly a third generated in the EMEA region, complemented by smaller activities in the wider Asian region and Canada and Latin America.

With customer requirements on the increase, scale is of importance, with AON being strong in data, analytics, innovation, and capital, all of which require scale to deliver on synergies and better insights.

The company has seen solid improvements over the past decade, having grown revenues from less than $10 billion a decade ago to $16 billion by now. Margins have increased, now exceeding a quarter of sales, as the company bought back nearly one in every four shares over the past decade, all resulting in very strong achievements on a per-share basis.

This has been well reflected in the share price, as a $100 stock in 2015 broke the $300 mark in 2021, hit highs just over $400 earlier this year, now trading around $350 per share.

The Base Case

In January of this year, AON reported a 17% increase in 2024 sales to $15.7 billion, with adjusted operating margins being very strong at 31.5% of sales, allowing for a 10% increase in adjusted earnings to $15.60 per share. Organic growth was reported at 6%, with the growth largely being driven by a $13 billion acquisition of NFP, which closed in April 2024. As a result, debt incurred made it so that improvements on the bottom line trailed the top-line sales trends.

Revenues from risk capital came in at $10.5 billion, notably from commercial risk and, to a smaller extent, reinsurance solutions, these showing solid high single digit organic growth rates. Human capital revenues rose by more than 30%, aided by the NFP deal announced in 2023, with total revenues of $5.2 billion now making up a third of total sales.

Based on current price levels, shares trade at 22 times earnings reported for 2024. Given the pace of organic growth and the fact that NFP only starts to contribute in full to the 2025 results, naturally growth was seen in 2025.

Amidst optimism in the business, the company hiked its dividend by 10% in April, now payable at $2.98 per share each year, for a dividend yield still trailing 1%, but this is a nice increase in the dividend nonetheless.

Momentum continued in the first half of the year, with reported sales up 16% and organic revenues up 5%. Second quarter sales were up 11%, amidst a solid 6% increase in organic sales, with adjusted earnings so far this year up sixty-seven cents on the year before, making a $17 per share number likely reasonable for this year.

Net debt is reported at $15.9 billion, yet with adjusted EBIT (this not being a very asset-intensive business) trending around $6 billion, leverage ratios are seen in the higher 2s, perfectly manageable, also considering the defensive nature of the operations.

With 217 million shares trading around $350 per share, the equity valuation of the firm stands at $76 billion, for a $92 billion enterprise valuation. This values the business at just over 5 times sales, seen exceeding $17 billion this year.

Leveraging Down

Early in September, AON announced that it had reached a deal to sell some of NFP's wealth business, including Wealthspire Advisors, Fiducient Advisors, Newport Private Wealth, to its former owners, Madison Dearborn Partners. The activities are sold in a $2.7 billion deal, with net cash proceeds seen at $2.2 billion, indicating that this is somewhat of a bolt-on sale.

With the business generating about $127 million in EBITDA, the multiple fetched comes in at around 21 times EBITDA ahead of taxes, and 17 times EBITDA based on the net cash proceeds, which frankly looks favorable with the own business trading around 15 times EBITDA. This furthermore allows for some net debt reduction, with pro forma net debt seen below $14 billion, reducing leverage towards, or just below, 2.5 times EBITDA here.

Reaching Fair Valuations

With earnings easily seen around $16-17 per share, earnings multiples have come down to 20-22 times earnings, as shares have seen long term returns, but frankly trade at levels seen early in 2022 already, marking some underperformance in recent years.

This has resulted in valuation multiple deflation, with these multiples starting to look more compelling, as I like the combination of organic growth, M&A and buybacks, with the latest divestment looking to take place at fair valuations, reducing leverage, which is very acceptable.

This results in the relative appeal rapidly increasing, as I think that a small premium to the market is warranted given the strong track record, as current levels start to look compelling, making me interested to get involved on further dips in the lower $300s.

data.title: QQQI: A Tax-Efficient Income Generator, But Investors Need To Understand The Nuances
data.description:
Today, ETFs are so diverse and super-specialized that it can be hard for an investor to find one that fits their portfolio but doesn't conflict with other parts that have different goals. The truth is, it's not really a conflict unless your goal is singular. For example, a portfolio primarily focused on capital growth isn't going to perform as well with an income ETF attached to it, because that would reduce the portfolio's capacity to generate unrealized gains.

But what if there's an alternative approach to portfolio creation that can capture the best of all worlds?

There's another way of thinking that's more nuanced, and it is that each sub-structure within a portfolio can co-exist with other sub-structures, each one doing its part to ultimately achieve a balanced aggregate goal. The 60/40 formula doesn't seem to work for most investors, and why should it? Investment choices are no longer straightforward, so why should your portfolio be plain vanilla when there are securities for nearly every consideration - capital appreciation, dividend growth, ongoing income, downside protection, tail-risk protection, favorable tax treatment, etc.?

These goals aren't necessarily contradictory, and I believe they can be used to achieve a much more beneficial goal that in the end still serves its original purpose - and that is to make your money work for you.

What is the NEOS NASDAQ-100 High Income ETF?

I'm going to be looking at just one of these components of a robust portfolio using a vehicle called the NEOS NASDAQ-100 High Income ETF (NASDAQ:QQQI), incepted in January 2024. This fund gives investors a monthly payout that is tax-efficient, and a key phrase to keep in mind here is 'return of capital', which we'll get into in more detail. For now, suffice it to understand that QQQI generates income that can be beneficial from a taxation point of view.

The fund's expense ratio is 0.68%, and that's to be expected from an active fund. The 'active' part of that refers to the options strategy that's paired with a majority equity investment as well as active rebalancing of the equities, the equities in question being the constituents of the Nasdaq 100 Index (NDX) that are replicated in the ETF's holdings. This comprises about 80% of the fund's NAV at a minimum, at any given time. Usually a lot more.

One major difference (among others) between QQQI and other covered call or buy-write ETFs is that the calls they sell are not 'covered', the reason being the options are sold on NDX rather than its equity components. That's a significant distinction because, in addition to the income that the premiums bring in, there's also capital appreciation from the equity holdings - not to mention dividends thereof. This is important to understand because conventional covered call strategies tend to cap upside on whatever portion of the equities the options are written against.

The distributions you get from QQQI are "classified as a return of capital and may be comprised of option premiums, dividends, capital gains, and interest payments", and this benefit arises from the NDX options contracts, which are classified as “Section 1256 Contracts”. Under the pertinent tax rule, any gains on these contracts that are held during the year or at the end of the tax year get the 60/40 capital gains tax treatment, which is 60% taxed at long-term cap gains rates and 40% as short-term cap gains rates. It doesn't matter how long the contracts are held for.

The fund also uses losses from both the equity and options positions to offset gains from equities and options contracts that did well over the year. Tax loss harvesting, in other words.

But how does that benefit investors? Well, it's a benefit as well as a drawback because the tax on return of capital is deferred, meaning it doesn't fall due at the end of the tax year. What it does is reduce your cost basis for the shares you hold, and this is where the drawback comes from because when you finally sell the shares, your gains are outsized because the cost basis is now considerably lower than when you bought them.

Many investors may not be aware of this when they look at the enticing 14.4% distribution rate. The return of capital as a selling point is not as well-understood, but it's simply a matter of kicking the tax can down the road. In another sense, the longer you hold the ETF the more strongly you're locked in, because selling your shares later rather than sooner would mean owing a higher amount in taxes. Since your gains are calculated against the cost basis, and the cost basis itself keeps reducing over time, that's something you should be prepared for.

Your tax advisor should be able to give you a much clearer picture than I can, but that's the gist of it.

A Look at QQQI's Performance and Distributions

Knowing all that, let's look at how the fund has performed since its inception in January 2024. Please note that total return can never be lower than price return, so don't get confused by the graph. The two graph lines are recorded on different y-axes (left for price and right for total.)

What might be apparent is that NAV per share growth has been muted, but that's normal because, if you'll recall, the return of capital distributions, which comprise the majority of the annual payouts, are being paid from net assets that include your investment.

A distribution will reduce the Fund’s NAV per Share and may be taxable to you as ordinary income or capital gain even though, from an economic standpoint, the distribution may constitute a return of capital.

These ROC distributions will reduce your cost basis, yes, but you still need to pay tax on those when you eventually sell. However, the timing of your investment is key, which is why the fund also advises investors to try and avoid investing right before a distribution is made.

You may wish to avoid investing in the Fund shortly before a dividend or other distribution, because such a distribution will generally be taxable even though it may economically represent a return of a portion of your investment. Distributions in excess of the Fund’s current and accumulated earnings and profits are treated as a tax-free return of your investment to the extent of your basis in the Shares, and generally as capital gain thereafter.

It makes sense to me because when you invest before a distribution, any profits earned by the fund until that point are taxable to you even though they're classified as ROC from an economic standpoint, and this taxable portion could be higher because it's being calculated on a lower post-distribution NAV.

I don't know if I'm explaining this well enough, so please talk to your tax advisor before you invest in QQQI. I'm not a tax professional but this is how I understand it.

Coming to the total return, the 36% gain in about seven quarters needs to be measured against the total return of a relevant benchmark like the Nasdaq-100 Total Return index, and this is what that looks like since the fund's inception.

This chart perfectly captures the investment case for QQQI, because it forces you to ask yourself: do I want pure capital appreciation only with all dividends reinvested, or am I looking for monthly income but possibly willing to give up some TR performance by not reinvesting?

Keep in mind that it's not an insignificant difference, and one that can compound over time. Meanwhile the clock keeps ticking on your tax deferment so you may end up paying a lot more in long-term capital gains on QQQI, since your cost basis keeps reducing.

Definitely something to think about.

Scenarios Where This May Be Beneficial

In the end, the investment decision comes down to how such a component can benefit your overall portfolio.

One scenario where this might work well is if your portfolio is already subject to a large amount of short-term capital gains from other dividend stocks you hold that is treated as ordinary income or non-qualified dividends. Since your QQQI dividends are ROC and therefore tax-deferred, it's like complementing your core holding with a tax-efficient accompaniment that gives you a monthly payout that's not immediately taxable to you.

In another scenario, QQQI would be a better holding than QQQ because your total return would be very similar, give or take but you're getting a large portion as your ROC distribution, thereby deferring your tax obligation until it's time to sell. Even then, they'd be considered as long-term capital gains with relatively favorable rates attached to them.

Here's some additional color around that. These are the tax percentages for long- and short-term capital gains for various tax brackets.

You can see how this might be favorable if some of your income qualifies for long-term capital gains tax. Again, speak to a tax advisor to see where you fit into this matrix and whether or not QQQI can help bring your short-term tax liabilities into a lower tax bracket.

Risks to Keep in Mind

The biggest risk I see is that the equities in this ETF are generally considered to be expensive and valuation-stretched. They're also more volatile than (SPY), the accepted proxy for the S&P 500 Index (SP500).

Another risk comes from the options strategy, which may not provide sufficient income to offset the distributions, thereby affecting net asset value over time.

We've seen that happen once already, as I explain below, and it could well happen again if the index keeps rallying further between now and October 17. Please read on to find out why that's an important date.

To elaborate, this risk comes from the fact that NDX options are cash-settled at expiration because there aren't any underlying shares to be called away like in a traditional covered call setup. If the index level has moved significantly higher than the strike price at the time of contract expiration, the seller needs to settle up with the buyer, and if that exceeds the premiums collected, that's a net loss position.

I'm going to go deeper into that because I think it's a primary risk factor with this ETF. NDX options are attractive because the premiums are high, the reason being each contract represents a 100 units of NDX, so the notional amount of each contract is 100x the level of the index. You can see this in absolute numbers in the Annual Report for the year ended May 31, 2025.

The calculations are as follows. As of Friday, May 30, 2025, NDX was at this level.

If you take that and multiply it by the 286 contracts held and then again by 100 units per contract, you get the notional value recorded against the 22,150 strike price for those contracts, or $610,352,314.

And that's where the risk lies - the scale of the leverage employed.

NDX shot up above the 22,150 strike price by July 18, so the fund essentially made a loss on that position, and an even bigger loss on the other set of contracts with the 21,800 strike price.

The fund hasn't disaggregated the premiums collected on each set of contracts, but here's the loss formula, which is the cash settlement on the day after contract expiration:

Cash settlement - equal to the difference between the final settlement value and the strike price of the contract, multiplied by $100

The higher NDX lands at expiration, the higher the cash settlement, so if that amount is higher than the premiums received, it's a net loss.

At the moment (file needs to be downloaded), QQQI holds October 17 calls written at strike levels of 24,250 and 23,925. With NDX currently well over the 24,000 level, there's every likelihood that this will result in a net loss position.

That's why this risk is so important to understand.

A further risk is one that I already mentioned about your long-term capital gains as a percentage of your cost basis being higher in the case of QQQI over QQQ.

And that rounds up our discussion of the QQQI ETF. I'm not making a recommendation because this is part of Seeking Alpha's ETF Education Series, but I hope this helps with your due diligence.

This article answers these three main questions about QQQI:

  • How does QQQI fit with other investments to form a well-rounded portfolio?
  • Are there specific tax situations with QQQI that need to be taken into consideration?
  • What type of investor does QQQI appeal to the most?

Editor's Note: This article is intended to provide a general overview of the ETF for educational purposes only and, unlike other articles on Seeking Alpha, does not offer an investment opinion about the ETF.

data.title: S&P 500: P/E And Interest Rates Scare Me More Than Record Buffett Indicator
data.description:
Buffett indicator sets new record

Many equity investors follow the so-called Buffett indicator (BI) for a good reason. Most good ideas in investing are simple ideas and the BI is certainly one of them. The BI is simply defined as the ratio of the total market capitalization of the stock market in a country to the country’s GDP and provides an assessment of the overall valuation of the stock market. Intuitively, when the BI is far above 100%, it indicates the stock market is at the risk of overvaluation relative to the country’s GDP.

This simple concept has demonstrated remarkable predictive power in the past, as you can see from the chart below during both the dot.com bubble and the 2008 crisis. In both cases, the BI has pushed noticeably above 100% and sharp correction in the stock market followed shortly. With such historical data, investors have good reasons to be concerned about the current BI. As seen in the chart above, the BI now hovers around 216%, setting a new historical high.

With this background, the thesis of this article is threefold. First, I will explain why the calculation of the BI (or the interpretation) may have become outdated since Warren Buffett first introduced it. Second, I will suggest some other indicators for investors to gauge the current equity market such as the Shiller CAPE ratio and the excess CAPE yield. Third, I will suggest a few alternative ideas in today’s market for investors to consider.

Why the BI need an updated interpretation

The fundamental reason that could render the BI outdated in today’s market is that many large U.S. companies have global business footprints. When Buffett first introduced this indicator, the world was much less interconnected and digital economy played a far pronounced role. Today, the stock market (either approximated by the Dow (DJI), the S&P 500 (SP500), or the NASDAQ index (COMP:IND)) are dominated by companies that have global reach and derive large portions of their revenues from digital sales. As an example, if you only look at the table below, you will see all the major market indices were heavily dominated by about the same set of stocks (mostly the Magnificent Seven). The top 10 stocks make up about 1/3 of the total market represented by the VTI ETF, almost 38% of the SP500 represented by the VOO ETF, and more than 52% of the NASDAQ index approximately by the QQQ ETF.

International sales have been the main drivers of market cap growth for all the mag 7 stocks in recent years. Take Nvidia (NVDA) and Microsoft (MSFT), the top 2 most valuable companies, as an example. The chart below shows that NVDA derives only 47% of its revenues from the U.S., and the majority of its revenues from overseas. The picture of MSFT is quite similar with a roughly 50:50 split as seen. All told, a recent Goldman Sachs report shows that on average, overseas sales made up about 28% of S&P 500 revenues in 2024.

Current Market Valuation calculates the current Buffett Indicator at 217% by dividing the market cap of $65.47 trillion by the GDP of $30.15 trillion. If the market capitalization were reduced by $10 trillion to reflect the fact that half of the Magnificent Seven's revenue comes from overseas, the Buffett Indicator would drop to 183%. If all information technology stocks were factored in, the Buffett Indicator would decline even more significantly.

With such a significant overseas exposure, dividing the stock market cap by the US GDP (BTW, the D here stands for domestic) could overestimate the valuation risks of the US equity market.

Alternative indicators: Shiller CAPE and excess CAPE yield

However, just because the BI could need an updated interpretation does not mean the market is not overvalued. The two indictors I believe are more relevant that the BI in today’s market are the Shiller CAPE ratio and also the excess CAPE yield. Both concepts are detailed in Dr. Robert Shiller’s book Irrational Exuberance and here I will only provide the background on an as-need basis to stay on point.

The Shiller CAPE is calculated based on the SP500’s 10-year inflation-adjusted P/E ratio and thus filters out short-term EPS fluctuations. As seen in the chart below, this ratio is currently around 39.60x. This is alarming in my mind for at least 2 reasons. First, it is among the highest levels in the index’s recorded history. As seen, the last time – and the only time since the 1880s - the ratio exceeded the current level was during the peak of the dot-com bubble. Before that, the CAPE ratio rarely exceeded 30x, with the market’s temporary peak before the Great Depression and shortly after the epic easing during the COVID pandemic as two notable exceptions.

Secondly, the current CAPE ratio is also alarming when benchmarked against risk-free rates, which acts as gravity on all assets valuation (another simply and effective wisdom from Warren Buffett). As a result, today, with 10-year treasury rates (US10Y) hovering above 4%, the equity market faces far greater valuation risks compared to 2021, when the CAPE ratio hovered around a similar level but 10-year treasury rates were only around 1%.

The concept that captures the above interplay between the CAPE ratio and risk-free rate is the so-called CAPE excess yield. It is simply calculated as the difference between the CAPE yield of SP500 and real 10-year treasury rates. As seen from the chart below, historical data suggests a strong correlation between the excess yield and the future return of the SP500. Per the latest data point provided by Dr. Shiller, the excess CAPE yield is about 1.5% only, amongst the thinnest levels ever observed. Even with an aggressive assumption of 3x interest cuts ahead, the projected excess yield will be around 2.25% at SP500’s current valuation and inflation, still quite thin by historical standard.

Long-term rates vs. short-term rates

Actually, for the above scenario to work, we need two more assumptions - equally aggressive in my view as the 3x rate cuts assumption. First, we will need to assume inflation remains at the current level and don’t further increase. And second, we will need to assume that long-term treasury rates respond in tandem with short-term rate cuts. After all, the Fed only controls short-term rates and long-term rates are controlled by market forces. As a recent example, 10-year rates actually increased and diverged from 3-month rates (at least thus far) after the Fed announced the rate cuts in mid-September as you can see from the following chart. Looking ahead, many other factors could cause similar divergence including investors’ concern about the U.S. debt burden and credit ratings, which in turn could pressure the CAPE excess yield.

Other risks and final thoughts

Finally, inflation is another uncertainty. Inflation seems to be under control per the most recent data. However, many factors (such as trade disputes) could drive up inflation and thus drive down the real-10-year treasury rates, which in turn would further pressure the already-thin CAPE excess yield mentioned above. Given how elevated the market valuation is now and also the many undergoing geopolitical conflicts, I suggest investors err on the side of being conservative.

My way of being conservative in this case is simply to monitor the yield spread between CAPE yield and 10-year treasury rates. This method eliminates the uncertainties caused by inflation data and my compilation of historical data is shown in the chart below. The current yield spread is about -1.63% only (with a CAPE ratio of 39.6x and a 10-year treasury rate of 4.16%). As seen, this spread is also among the thinnest levels since the peak of the dot.com bubble.

Let me close by offering a few alternative investing ideas besides U.S. equity. To start, we suggest investors consider ex-U.S. exposure. Back to the BI, as the BI for the U.S. market sets new record, the BI for other major economies is quite reasonable as seen in the next chart. Furthermore, many of their company are more domestically oriented than their U.S. peers. Therefore, the application of the BI could provide a more accurate assessment of their equity valuation. For example, China, the 2nd largest global economy with a GDP of $18.91 Trillion, has a BI of about 73% only. Similarly, Japan, the 3rd largest global economy with a GDP of $4.07 Trillion, has a BI of about 53.6% only, the lowest among the 7 countries with largest GDP. Other major economies like UK and France also feature BI close to 100%. Investors could consider corresponding ETFs like MCHI, EWG, EWU, and EWQ.

For us, we find the use of VEU simple and effective enough from past experience. As you can see from the last chart below, which displays our actual holding our growth portfolio, we hold a sizable position of VEU with about 13.5% allocation. As seen, we also supplement/diversify our US market exposure with considerable amounts of tactical holdings with more reasonable valuation and also alternative assets such as precious metal. In particular, we see an ideal setup (what we call a golden triangle) that can push precious metal price further up despite their large rallies lately: geopolitical uncertainty, inflation concerns, and expectations of lower interest rates. This article is already getting long-winded and thanks for staying with me to this point. I will stop here and leave more elaborations for later.

data.title: Data Centers And AI Are Micron's Edge
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I'll cut to the chase; Micron Technology (NASDAQ: NASDAQ:MU) is firing on all cylinders and still seriously cheap. Since my last coverage, the company has had its share price increase by 30%. All the same, one of the reasons why I see MU as a cheap stock relates to its 4Q FY2025 Earnings results, where the company recorded revenues of $37.4Bn, a 50% revenue growth. And this has been majorly attributed to the rock-solid DRAM pricing across markets. Additionally, booming AI and data center sales have also played a part in ensuring that the company is improving its margins and revenue growth. I note that AI is not a one quarter fluke here. In fact, management guidance into the future, FY2026 shows that data center is a key factor driving growth as noted by the CEO;

...We have strong momentum entering fiscal 2026 with a robust fiscal Q1 demand outlook led by data center and the most competitive position in our history. Over the coming years, we expect trillions of dollars to be invested in AI and a significant portion will be spent on memory...

Sanjay Mehrotra, CEO & President 4Q FY2025 Earnings Call

With the demand for data center and AI compute on the rise, I still see the demand for HBM and high-capacity DRAM increasing significantly into FY2026.

...expect healthy demand supply environment in 2026 for overall DRAM, and that bodes well for profitability of DRAM, profitability of HBM and of course, profitability of non-HBM as well, which is experiencing tight supply....

And for these reasons, I maintain my buy rating for Micron Technology.

Business Overview

I have followed Micron Technology for quite a while. And I would say that as of writing this, the story is finally catching up with the numbers. First and foremost, MU is one of the world's leading makers of memory chips especially DRAM. And obviously, beyond smartphones, PCs, servers and mobiles, MU has been aggressively focusing towards AI and data center chips. And this is because of the growth that is expected from the semiconductor space. In my previous coverage titled " Micron Technology: Gearing For AI Market With HBM4" I talked about how growth for semiconductors related to AI is expected to be 5X than growth in the remainder of the market. I also elaborated why high memory bandwidth is needed and required for many AI applications. Now, as I have mentioned the numbers are finally coming together. As of 4Q FY2025, Cloud Memory Business Unit finally hit a record number. The Cloud Memory Business Unit revenue was $4.5Bn and represented 40% of the total company revenue. On the other side, HBM sales reached a new quarterly record as many companies and hyperscalers race to train larger AI models. And this means that Micron isn't just selling commodity laptops chips anymore. In fact, it is building the backbone memory for large companies such as Nvidia (NVDA) and Google's (GOOG) (GOOGL) AI data centers;

...In close collaboration with NVIDIA, Micron has pioneered the adoption of LPDRAM for servers, and since NVIDIA's launch of LPDRAM in their GB-product family Micron has been the sole supplier of LPDRAM in the data center.

Sanjay Mehrotra, CEO & President 4Q FY2025 Earnings Call

And remember it still owns a larger market share for older markets and $100Bn for HBM Market by 2028.

Growth and Profitability

When it comes to growth and profitability, I note a couple of things. First and foremost, the growth grade still stands at a solid A+ grade which I would say its quite impressive. Given that it has maintained the A+ grade for the last 6 months.

And this is obviously because of its impressive quarterly numbers. In 4Q FY2025, revenue was 50% higher than 2024. And Q4 alone was $11.3Bn which is a 46% y/y increase driven by record data center revenues and strong sequential growth. I note that, the demand for DRAM is quite high especially after hitting $9Bn in Q4. And what's even better is that Micron converted all that revenue into significant profit. The gross margins exploded to 41% in 4Q FY2025.

And this was because of better pricing execution and a favorable product mix as noted.

...Prices increased in the low double-digit percentage range, driven by tight industry DRAM supply, pricing execution and favorable mix....

So what are the growth drivers moving forward?

Growth Drivers

From the 4Q FY2025, earnings data, it is easy to deduce the catalysts moving forward: AI driven memory demand. And this will be coupled with a tight global supply given that chips are becoming scarce again. In my previous coverage, I said that every AI farm requires a heavy dose of RAM and HBM. And this obviously requires money. A lot of it.

...we expect trillions of dollars to be invested in AI and a significant portion will be spent on memory. As the only U.S.-based manufacturer of memory, Micron is uniquely positioned to benefit from the AI opportunity ahead. Thank you for joining us today. We will now open for questions...

Sanjay Mehrotra, CEO & President 4Q FY2025 Earnings Call

Having said that, I note that Micron is seated at the driver's seat of this megatrend. First, because it has a leading edge technology (such as the 1-gamma DRAM, the mature HBM3E Chips and the next-gen G9 NAND). Secondly I note that Micron already has a sold-out order book. And this means that orders and demand extends into 2026. And lastly, is its scaling capacity which I would say aligns with future needs as observed.

...we received a CHIPS grant disbursement following the completion of a key construction milestone for our new high-volume manufacturing fab in Idaho...

In addition, I also note that DRAM and NAND manufacturing have gone into an undersupply. This clearly implies that pricing of these products will be affected and as a result, I expect prices to rise. Which will then translate into profitability for MU.

Valuation

In terms of valuation, there are a couple of things to note. First, I still believe that given its high growth, the market has only priced in a portion of this growth in terms of its price. Obviously, at a FWD Non-GAAP PEG ratio of 0.15x against sector median of 1.83x, then for sure I believe the 91% discount is realistic relative to the sector. Again, when I look at the FWD Non-GAAP P/E, I note that MU is trading at 9.81x while the sector median stands at 25.57x which shows a 61% discount. Lastly, as shown below, from an EV.EVITDA Perspective, MU still trades at a 63% discount relative to the sector. While Micron is trading at a FWD EV/EBITDA Multiple of 5.70x, the sector median stands at 15.52x.

Similar to my prior analysis, the company still trades at a discount relative to most of its peers in terms of EV/EBITDA. And this makes me believe that Micron warrants a buy rating given the reasons, I have explained prior.

Micron's Risks

In as much there are a lot of positives for MU, there is still a lot that could go wrong. First, memory is a boom-burst kind of business. And if AI demand flattens or decreases, then of course prices could reduce as it were the case in 2018. Secondly, I still see competition risk as a big risk for MU. And this is because of the number of players that want to ride the AI wave. This is not to forget that big players such as Samsung (OTCPK:SSNLF) and Hynix (OTCPK:HXSCF) are still in the race especially now that they are ramping up their HBM3 technology.

Final Thoughts

In my initial coverage I initiated a buy rating on MU. And this has now been confirmed by the 4Q FY2025 Earnings results. I would say that my conviction has been validated. And the market is still treating MU like an old cyclical player (obviously due to its traditional business model around laptops, mobile phones etc.) and not considering the AI and data center side of things. And honestly, that's both the edge and Leverage that Micron Technology has. And for these reasons, I reiterate my Buy rating.

data.title: LVHD: A Yield Trap In Disguise
data.description:
The Franklin U.S. Low Volatility High Dividend Index ETF (NASDAQ:LVHD) makes an interesting argument of providing investors with high dividends and low volatility. In theory, this is a very strong value proposition, as the current market cycle is marked by a lot of uncertainty and that managing risks is becoming increasingly important as valuations are at record highs.

Indeed, LVHD provides investors with higher than the benchmark dividends and lower valuations, but as many similar ETFs, when you actually look at risk-adjusted returns, you find out that even if you take less risks, you are not remunerated enough for them, and you are actually suffering an opportunity cost. This is the case for LVHD, and for the risk level of the fund, you are not remunerated enough. I rate LVHD a hold, as it fails to provide investors with alpha over its benchmark the Russell 3000, that we will use as a comparative with the iShares Russell 3000 ETF (IWV).

Portfolio Composition and Sector Concentration

Looking at LVHD’s key features, it will directly tell you what kind of fund it is. The fund holds 118 companies with a top 10 concentration of 26.13%. As it is based on the Russell 3000, the fund makes strict selections but still comes out diversified in terms of individual holdings. Looking at the sectoral split, there is a domination of utilities and consumer staples, both above 20%: sectors known for their defensive settings and high dividend distributions, as well with a 15% stake in real estate. This makes sense with the fund promise, and contrasts with IWV’s focus on IT for more than 30% of the fund, followed by financials and consumer discretionary with a top 10 concentration of 33.92 for 2587 companies. Two very different funds.

To construct its dividend focused portfolio, LVHDs index, the Franklin Low Volatility High Dividend Index screens Russell 3000 companies for profitable companies, with the potential to pay high and sustainable dividends. Yields are then scored based on the attractiveness of their price and earnings volatility. Highest scoring securities are kept and they apply concentration limits: no holding above 2.5%, no sector above 25%, and REIT companies can’t exceed 15%.

This results in a logical focus on utilities, staples, and real estate according to concentration limits but also means that the fund is little to not exposed to growth found in sectors like tech (2.64%) or industrials (5.83%), where most of the market return is. In addition, I find it surprising to see a high portfolio turnover of 49%, meaning that the fund’s portfolio will be entirely different every two years, and this is not coherent with the fund’s goal of “equity securities with high dividends, low volatility and sustainable earnings”. If the portfolio rotates so much, it means that the earnings of companies selected were not that sustainable in the first place, or they would have been kept in the portfolio.

As IWV is tracking the market it has a large exposure to these industries, mirroring market cap weights but also being riskier. However, risk is not always a bad thing if it is remunerated properly, we will get back to it later.

Before saying bad things about LVHD (and I will) one important thing to note is that the fund is quite efficient in preserving capital. Over the last 5 years, the fund’s maximum drawdown was only -13.29%, very little compared to IWV’s -24.71%. This is a good thing, but it doesn’t compensate for the other significant tradeoffs in performance.

Financial Metrics

In terms of expense, I actually think that LVHD is not expensive. The fund’s expense ratio is only 0.27%, and when you factor in the niche positioning, the high portfolio turnover, and the fact that IWV, the largest Russell 3000 ETF, has a 0.20% expense ratio, a 0.07% premium seems reasonable. The fund’s liquidity is also not an issue, LVHD is not a very large fund and manages $596.1 Mn, a dwarf against IWV’s $17.0 Bn, but this is enough to show a median bid/ask spread of 0.05%, acceptable but that should be monitored. The daily trading volume of $2.6 Mn is not that large and the spread could widen if a large transaction were to occur.

The fund’s dividend yield is naturally above benchmark: 3.32% backed by a 30-day SEC yield of 3.52% against 0.97% for IWV. The dividend promise is fulfilled. If we look at valuations, as LVHD focuses on defensive sectors with mature companies paying dividends, the fund’s P/E ratio is only 17.72 against 28.71 for its investment universe. The same thing goes for the P/B ratio: 2.36 against 4.80.

That’s for the dividend argument, now let’s look at the volatility argument. The fund’s standard deviation is 14.66% (3 years) and 14.30% (5 years). Compared to IWV, this is 0.63 percentage points below for the 3 year period and 2.07 percentage points below for the 5 years period. So yes, LVHD is less volatile, but the difference is not really impressive.

Turning to the risk-adjusted performance, we will look at both funds’ Sharpe ratios. For LVHD: 0.19 (3 years) and 0.53 (5 years), against 0.88 (3 years) and 0.69 (5 years) for IWD. On a risk adjusted basis, LVHD is clearly underperforming.

Let’s look at total returns:

LVHD is underperforming in all time horizons, with large gaps each time. At this point, you would be better off buying IWV and boosting your distributions and lowering your volatility with short term risk-free bonds. You would reach the same level of volatility with higher returns, and a virtually zero portfolio turnover.

Hope for the future?

The sectoral split I mentioned earlier is 60% concentrated in utilities, consumer staples, and real estate. This is a positioning that makes sense given the focus on dividend, but we also saw that it is not working. This was a backward looking approach, but what can we expect from the future?

The first thing that I would like to note about sectors, is that during the 2020 market crash, information technology, consumer staples, and healthcare were the sectors that outperformed. I will not mention healthcare for obvious reasons, as the crash was due to the pandemic, but a direct conclusion would be that a defensive portfolio must be strong in IT and strong in consumer staples. LVHD is indeed strong in consumer staples, but not in IT, and this is not a good point given that there are IT companies with stable dividends, just not as high as 3.32%, but seeking dividends despite a lower total return is not a good idea without a proper risk reduction, that we are not seeing here. The lack of IT really is the dark spot of the fund, 2.64% versus IWV’s 31.90% is very slim and prevents the fund from benefiting from the primary driver of U.S. equity returns over the past half-decade. Same goes for industrials, to a lesser extent.

We could say that due to the high portfolio turnover, the sectoral split could change, but the rotation of the portfolio mostly happens within sectors, as the sectors represented the most are the ones distributing dividends. Looking at individual names doesn’t make sense, but I think the sectoral split shouldn't change too much.

I don’t think that LVHD would hold better than the market in case of a market crash (and at this point this is the only remaining appeal of the fund), because the sectors are facing challenges. Utilities, due to still high interest rates and inflation will face regulated price pressures if a recession happens, but this is partly offset by renewable energy investments. In this case, I would rather have a focus on renewable linked utilities with strong balance sheets, which is not what LVHD does. Same thing for consumer staples that will see pressure on their margins, even if private consumption appears to be resilient enough. Finally, REITs, mostly mortgage and retail property, are fighting the volatility induced by rate changes and the key to navigate the sector is to get exposure to prime assets in key locations with favorable growth dynamics, not necessarily those in LVHD’s portfolio as the only criteria is dividend.

In short, I don’t think that the fund’s sectoral positioning is bad, but the selection process results in a weak portfolio, as the high portfolio turnover shows despite the focus on companies that should be stable enough to stay longer than 2 years on the fund.

LVHD is aimed at providing stable income with low volatility, if this is what you are looking for, I don’t think this is the right ETF. I reaffirm what I said before: you would be better off with a combination of IWV and risk-free assets.

Risks

The key risk with LVHD is the opportunity cost. You might be tricked into thinking that the fund is effective: after all you get dividends and a lower volatility than the benchmark. The problem is that for the level of volatility you are accepting, your total returns should be higher. LVHD is not an effective fund.

In addition, despite a disciplined diversification on single companies, 60% of the fund is investing in 3 sectors. They are indeed defensive sectors, but I don’t have confidence in the ability of the fund to select the most resilient companies, and the concentration means exposure to regulatory shock, commodity spikes, or sector rotation.

Liquidity is not a big red flag but should be monitored given the small daily trading volume, you can expect high spreads on some days, this is something you should keep in mind.

The fund is not very exposed to macroeconomic risks but it still has a P/E ratio close to 18, meaning that portfolio companies are reliant on future growth expectations more than on distributions. This means direct volatility induced by interest rates, and a correlation between LVHD and the economic sentiment.

Conclusion

LVHD is in appearance delivering on its promise: indeed compared to the benchmark you will get low volatility and high yield. However, LVHD is a classic example of a yield trap where the first look gives you a good impression, but a second look is enough to convince you that something is not right.

This “something is not right” for LVHD is the risk-adjusted performance. For 2 percentage points of standard deviation removed, you can’t slash performance by a factor of 3 (more or less depending on time horizons) and be happy with your investments.

LVHD is a hold, and I don’t think it will ever be a buy on my end.

data.title: Wintrust Financial Corporation Deserves More Upside
data.description:
Back in early October of last year, I decided to reaffirm Wintrust Financial Corporation (NASDAQ:WTFC) as a "Buy" candidate. My overall assessment of the company was that, even after easily outperforming the S&P 500 over the span of several months, and even though it had experienced some weakness in some areas, the balance sheet continued to grow, and shares looked attractive in light of the overall high asset quality that the company exhibited.

The decision to reaffirm it as a "Buy" candidate has played out nicely. Since then, the stock is up 27.5%, comfortably outperforming the 17.6% rise the S&P 500 experienced. And since I originally rated it a "Buy" in June 2023, the stock has risen 94.1%. By comparison, the market is up a little more than half that at 51.4%. Based on the data provided, we continue to see attractive growth. This includes the firm's balance sheet and its income statement. Given this combination of factors, while the stock is not exactly cheap, I do believe that it still justifies a soft "Buy" rating at this time.

WFTC is still a solid bank

The newest data that we have regarding Wintrust Financial Corporation covers through the second quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. During that time, deposits for the company came in at $55.82 billion. That represents a nice increase over the $52.51 billion that the company reported at the end of last year. It is worth noting, however, that there has been some change that came from brokered deposits. In fact, brokered deposits expanded from $3.60 billion to $4.38 billion.

If we strip this out from the equation, we would still get a rise in deposits of about 5.2%. So that's still solid. Those who follow my work closely know that I don't have much of an issue with broker deposits. I just acknowledge them as more expensive deposits that are not always reliable. The fact that organic deposits still increased nicely over the span of six months is certainly encouraging to me. And that helps offset the rise in broker deposit exposure in my book.

As deposits for the institution grew, so too did the value of loans. They expanded from $47.69 billion in 2024 up to $50.65 billion in the second quarter of this year. The greatest exposure that the company has here would be to the $16.39 billion of commercial loans on its books. But there are other areas of concentration as well, including the $13.29 billion dedicated to commercial real estate, the $8.32 billion of premium finance receivables under the property and casualty category, and the $8.51 billion of premium finance receivables under the life insurance category.

When possible, I always like to know what kind of exposure the banks that I am analyzing have to office assets. After all, high and rising vacancy rates throughout the United States are proving to be a challenge for many financial institutions. As of the end of the most recent quarter, about 12.05% of the firm’s commercial real estate portfolio involved office assets. That amounts to approximately $1.60 billion in total. However, when you do the math, that is still a fairly small portion of the overall loan portfolio. Having said that, we are starting to see some deterioration here. Specifically, the non-performing loan ratio has grown from 0.41% in the first quarter of this year to 1.19% today. And the loans still accruing that are between 30 and 90 days past due have jumped from 0.60% to 1.77%. So this is certainly disconcerting, to say the least.

The value of securities has increased recently. At $8.39 billion for the second quarter of this year, they beat out the $7.75 billion that the company had in 2024. Over the same period, cash and cash equivalents expanded from $4.87 billion to $5.27 billion. But this did come at a bit of a cost. Although, it's not exactly a large one. From the end of last year to the second quarter of this year, the value of debt that the company has expanded from $4.24 billion to $4.33 billion.

Moving on to the income statement, we have seen some really impressive results. In the first half of 2025, as an example, the company enjoyed $1.03 billion in net interest income. That's up materially from the $873.1 million reported one year earlier. This was even though the net interest margin for the company dipped from 3.56% to 3.55%. And it was driven by an increase in the company's balance sheet, as well as a reduction in the provision for credit losses from $61.7 million to $46.2 million. This follows a similar trend, with net interest income climbing from $1.72 billion in 2023 to $1.86 billion in 2024.

Non-interest income for the company has, unfortunately, decreased as of late. After going from $434.1 million in 2023 to $488.3 million last year, it has dropped, hitting $240.7 million in the first half of 2025 compared to the $261.7 million reported a year earlier. According to the data provided, this was primarily because of a plunge in "other" income from $71.6 million to $43.8 million. Digging deeper, this seems to have been because of a $20 million gain that the company booked last year associated with the sale of its Retirement Benefits Advisors division under its wealth management business. Even despite this, net profits managed to climb from $325.7 million in the first half of last year to $370.6 million at the same time this year.

If we assume that the rest of this year will look like the first half of the year did on a year-over-year basis, we can calculate that the company is trading at a price-to-earnings multiple of 11.8. To be honest with you, I prefer a reading of 10 or lower in this industry. However, as the chart above illustrates, only one of the five firms that I compared it to is cheaper than it is on this basis. In the next chart below, I did the same type of comparative analysis using the price-to-book and price-to-tangible-book multiples. On a price-to-book basis, three of the five firms were cheaper than it is, while on a price-to-tangible-book basis, that number dropped to two.

Normally, this kind of valuation for a bank would lead me to take a more cautious approach and rate it a "Hold." However, it is important to consider asset quality as well. I typically like to see a return on assets reading of 1% or greater. But as the first chart below illustrates, Wintrust Financial Corporation boasts a reading of 1.19%. That puts the company above any of the five firms that I compared it to. However, when it comes to return on equity, the comparative ranking of the company is considerably lower, with three of the five firms, illustrated in the second chart below, higher than it is. Having said that, the 12.07% reading that we get here is above the 10% minimum that I prefer to see in the industry.

I think it would be useful to also touch briefly on credit quality metrics. According to the FDIC, nationally chartered commercial banks have an average non-performing assets-to-total assets ratio right now of 0.51%. This specifically refers to those with assets of $1 billion or greater. By comparison, that number for Wintrust Financial Corporation is just 0.31%. This only adds to the bullish argument as I see it.

Takeaway

Based on all the data currently available, I would make the case that while the easy money has certainly been made, Wintrust Financial Corporation still offers investors an interesting opportunity. I think, because of how the company is priced, it does justify a bullish outlook. So for that reason, I don't mind rating it a soft "Buy" at this time.

data.title: The Impact On The Economy And Markets Of The Looming Government Shutdown
data.description:
The Role Of Congress

The US Congress, otherwise known as the Legislature, is one of three co-equal branches of government, along with the Executive Branch and the Judicial Branch.

Colloquially it is often said that the Legislature makes the laws, the Executive Branch enforces the laws and the Judiciary interprets the laws.

The role of Congress is to introduce and pass bills, which become law when signed by the President. They also control government taxation and spending.

Additionally, Congress provides oversight to ensure laws are implemented correctly, as well as having the power to confirm presidential appointments and declare war.

With all of these duties, Congress only has one legislative responsibility that they are required to fulfill each year, and that is to pass a budget. In actuality, however, it is only necessary for Congress to pass part of the budget, because, as will be described below, much of the government’s annual spending is already authorized by pre-existing laws.

The Federal Budget

The Federal Budget is comprised of three components; Mandatory Spending, Discretionary Spending and Interest Expense.

Breakdown of the Federal Government’s $6.8 Trillion Budget in Fiscal Year 2024

Source: Congressional Budget Office

Mandatory programs are the largest segment, representing 61% of the total budget, and include the big three entitlement programs of Social Security, Medicare and Medicaid, which are 22%, 13% and 9% of total spending, respectively.

Discretionary Spending is the next largest component at 27% of spending, and is broken into Non-Defense Spending at 14% and Defense Spending at 13%.

Last comes Interest Payments on the National Debt at 13% of total spending.

Mandatory Spending and Interest Payments do not require annual approval by Congress because they are already authorized by pre-existing laws.

Consequently, the only annual requirement for Congress is to pass legislation which covers the Discretionary Spending portion, which, again, covers 27% of Total Government Spending. This is done through passing 12 Appropriations Bills. These bills must become law by September 30th each year, in order to fund the government for the next fiscal year.

If the Appropriations Bills are not signed into law, then on October 1st a Government Shutdown occurs, because the government can not legally fund the agencies covered by the appropriations.

Given the importance of passing a budget, and it being the sole legally required legislative action of Congress, one would think that this usually gets done.

But one would be wrong.

Over the past thirty-three years, Congress has only passed into law all of the required Appropriations Bills twice, with the most recent being in FY 1997.

That means Congress has failed to complete its legislative duties in 31 of 33 years, or 94% of the time. Thus, Congress deals with the threat of a Government Shutdown almost every year.

Congress does have one out, however, to prevent a shutdown, which is to pass a Continuing Resolution. A Continuing Resolution is a temporary measure that extends funding at existing levels to allow lawmakers more time to reach an agreement. This is the most common practice in the legislature, and is often referred to as “kicking the can down the road.” This is how Congress delays making difficult legislative decisions.

As of September 25, 2025, with 5 days to go, Congress has not yet passed into law a single Appropriations Bill for FY2026. Nor have they signed a Continuing Resolution.

The threat of a Government Shutdown is imminent.

History of Government Shutdowns

The reason why a Government Shutdown is often a threat is due to the rules for passing the budget. Under law, Appropriations Bills require a simple majority to pass in the House of Representatives, but must have a Super Majority of 60 votes to pass in the Senate.

Since no party has held a Super Majority in the Senate since the 1960’s, bi-partisan support is required. This gives the minority party some leverage in budget negotiations in order to provide their votes to pass the budget legislation.

A discussion of the political posturing between the two parties in order to push their agendas, and how the minority party uses their leverage, is beyond the scope of this analysis. Instead, I intend to focus on what happens if a Government Shutdown occurs.

First, although Congress has only passed all of their Appropriations Bills by the September 30th deadline twice in thirty-three years, a Government Shutdown is still a rare event.

In that time period, there have been 6 Government Shutdowns, although two of them lasted for less than three days, usually over a weekend, and are really only considered technical shutdowns because they were essentially over before any cessation of government activities could begin.

The other 4 lasted an average of 19 days each, and will be discussed below.

The Economic Impact Of A Government Shutdown

While government spending represents roughly one quarter of GDP, the impact of a Government Shutdown would be much less, because only some of the government’s programs would be closed.

Mandatory Spending is not affected, so Social Security and Medicare benefits still go out.

The Treasury is still able to pay on its debt obligations.

Only departments that Congress has not funded would be impacted, and they cover 27% of Government Spending. The majority of federal employees, roughly 65% would continue working, since they are considered essential workers.

Importantly, furloughed government workers would receive backpay once the government reopens, thanks to the Government Employee Fair Treatment Act of 2019.

Goldman Sachs has estimated that a government shutdown might reduce growth by 0.15% per week, but it would only be temporary. Growth would rise by a similar amount once operations are reopened.

The Congressional Budget Office estimated that the 2018-2019 shutdown, which at 35 days was the longest on record, reduced GDP by $11 billion. However, $9 billion of that was recovered the following quarter.

Overall, the economic impact of a shutdown is modest, unless the shutdown lasts for an extended period of time

The Market Impact Of A Government Shutdown

Historically, Government Shutdowns have not had a negative impact on stocks, as the S&P 500 Index has gained an average of 5.5% over the ensuing 40-day period following the shutdown.

The chart below shows how the stock market initially declines around Government Shutdowns, but quickly rebounds.

Source: Yahoo Finance and Goldman Sachs Global Investment Research

On a longer-term basis, looking at the two most recent shutdowns, the market impact of the closure is but a blip.

Conclusion

Congress has one responsibility that is required each year, which is to pass a budget to fund the government for the following fiscal year.

History has shown that they aren’t very good at doing this, as 94% of the time they reach their September 30th deadline for passing all of their Appropriations Bills without completing the task.

Yet a Government Shutdown, which is threatened almost every year, also rarely occurs. Instead, Congress frequently “kicks the can down the road” by passing a Continuing Resolution, which is a temporary measure to buy them some time to address the tough legislative stumbling blocks.

This year seems to be no different, as Congress will return on September 29th with only 2 days to finish their job.

The political posturing will continue next week, with either the passage of another Continuing Resolution, or a Government Shutdown.

While the rhetoric and political posturing is rising, the financial markets are calm, as the situation has historically always gotten resolved in short order. Concerns about a shutdown may trigger some volatility, and some headline risk about disruptions in government services and furloughed government workers, but the fundamentals of the economy and monetary policy will have a longer lasting impact.

Traditionally, the markets have looked through this political situation.

One thing that is different this year, however, is that the Office of Management and Budget have told federal agencies that will be impacted by funding cutoffs to prepare for the potential permanent reduction in jobs. This might be considered as a negotiating tactic.

Additionally, if a shutdown occurs for an extended period of time, it could delay the release of critical government statistics, which will disrupt the regular flow of information that markets rely on.

data.title: Pet Pain For Investor Gain
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Zoetis Inc. (NYSE:ZTS) is fundamentally a companion animal business whose valuation is mostly derived from the higher-growth, higher-margin companion animal franchise, which focuses on dermatology, parasiticides, and osteoarthritis (OA) pain management for cats and dogs. While remaining a significant contributor of revenue, its livestock business does not have the same growth momentum; however, it still generates consistent cash flow and affords capital allocation flexibility for Zoetis as whole. Companion animal business already makes up about 68% of 2024 overall revenue and is projected to reach 71% by 2030 as the segment serves the growing (and aging) pet population across the globe. Therefore, Zoetis is well-positioned to benefit from both a faster revenue growth and structurally improving margins through mix shift.

Zoetis shares have enjoyed a significant valuation premium as investors have been (rightfully so) excited about their business fundamentals and secular tailwind of pet demographics. However, reports of serious adverse events and subsequent FDA notice slowed down the operating momentum of its important OA pain management segment, which is largely perceived as the critical growth driver. No additional safety concerns have emerged since the initial discovery but investor sentiment has soured on the prospect of Zoetis maintaining its growth momentum. Even though the rest of the companion animal portfolio (e.g. dermatology and parasiticides) maintained strong operating momentum over the past few quarters, investors overlooked those positives as they had their eyes fixated on the lagging OA pain management performance.

In order to soothe the fears of both owners and vets, Zoetis has updated the US Librela label in February 2025, produced an owner information sheet, and rolled out medical education programs to promote appropriate use. While these are good measures to help reduce future misuse cases and associated adverse events, benign real-world evidence readouts over the next 6-12 months will be critical to draw the fearful owners and vets back to Librela and Solensia and investors will be looking for any evidence of reaccelerating growth momentum in OA business. Zoetis retains the capital allocation flexibility afforded by both healthy free cash flow generation and pristine balance sheet with minimal debt.

While Zoetis is not as richly valued as before, its share price still reflects a base-case growth scenario that depends on a substantial growth contribution from OA business. The situation is more binary (and skewed to downside) than I am usually comfortable with. Even though Zoetis presents an attractive growth story coupled with structural margin expansion at a reasonable valuation, there will be a sizeable downside if the OA business encounters even small amounts of hiccups whereas an equivalent upside may not be achievable unless an exceptional adoption curve materializes based on real-world safety and efficacy signals. The fact that Zoetis maintains levers to create additional value through optimization of both its business structure (e.g. spinning off livestock business) and capital structure (e.g. selectively raising more debt to reduce the equity-heavy WACC) may provide a floor. However, a meaningful re-rating of the stock will require a blue-sky growth scenario that hinges not only on de-risking and reacceleration of the OA business, but also on sustained growth of the rest of the companion animal business where both parasiticides and dermatology franchises face intensifying competition.

Business Overview

Zoetis is the world’s largest pure play animal health company that serves both companion animals and livestock across more than 100 countries. In terms of geography, Zoetis organizes itself into US and international segments. In terms of species groups, Zoetis organizes itself into companion animals and livestock segments. In 2024, Zoetis generated about $9.3 billion in revenue with 68% coming from the faster-growing, higher-margin companion animal business – it had contributed only about 40% back in 2017. Given the prevalence of pet ownership in the US, companion animal revenue constituted 87% of US revenue in Q2 2025. International markets also lean heavily on companion animal business, but their weight is much less pronounced at 57% during the same period.

Zoetis has a broad product suite of eight major categories: parasiticides, dermatology, pain and sedation, other pharmaceuticals, diagnostics, vaccines, anti-infectives, and medicated feed additives. Dermatology, parasiticides, and pain and sedation (e.g. OA) categories bolster the growth of both companion animals and broader Zoetis' portfolio.

Dermatology is a category essentially created by Zoetis, and the company still remains the industry leader with Apoquel and Cytopoint. The Parasiticides franchise continues to generate strong operational growth as Simparica / Simparica Trio remain the industry standard. The Pain and sedation franchise is expected to drive the bulk of future growth spearheaded by Librela and Solensia, monoclonal antibody therapies that address chronic pain in aging dogs and cats. Zoetis has consistently invested about 7% of revenue in R&D over the past 10 years, advancing longer‑acting OA antibodies, lifecycle extensions in dermatology and parasiticides, and new formulations.

Zoetis is relatively well shielded against tariffs and trade volatility as its globally distributed network allows local sourcing. Approximately 75% of products sold in the US are manufactured domestically and the company maintains a net exporter position in the US. While no explicit dollar amount or margin impact was disclosed, management has incorporated tariffs into its 2025 guidance and has emphasized mitigation levers such as manufacturing footprint, sourcing flexibility, and pricing. The Competitive landscape includes Elanco Animal Health Inc. (ELAN), Boehringer Ingelheim GmbH (privately held), and Merck Animal Health through Merck & Co., Inc. (MRK).

OA Pain Deep Dive

OA warrants a separate deep dive not only because it represents a major growth driver – given its high prevalence and chronic underdiagnosis – but also because investor concerns have centered on the safety profile of its anti‑NGF (nerve growth factor) monoclonal antibodies. In dogs, radiographic evidence of OA is present in roughly 40% of the population, including younger cohorts, but only about 16–24% meet clinical definitions depending on pain threshold. In cats, OA is even more prevalent with clinical signs observed in about 40% of the overall population and radiographic evidence present in more than 90% of cats over 12 years old. The clinical signs are infrequently recognized by cat owners with only 13% of affected cats globally being diagnosed. Such disconnect between diagnosis and treatment creates a substantial latent market that could be unlocked by better screening, owner education, and easier treatment options.

For better screening, Zoetis' offers AI-powered Virtual Laboratory that can quickly supply expert-level support for diagnosis. Also, Zoetis management explicitly stated that "advances in diagnostics are helping unlock opportunity, enabling broader access, earlier intervention and better outcomes." Zoetis has also focused on providing owner education through publications including OA pain checklist that help dog owners recognize the signs of OA pain.

Finally, ZTS has developed a differentiated solution for OA with Librela (for dogs) and Solensia (for cats) that are once-monthly, species-specific monoclonal antibodies that neutralize nerve growth factor, interrupting a key pain signaling pathway without the GI and renal liabilities of chronic NSAID use or inconsistent efficacy seen with EP4 antagonists. Both Librela and Solensia are administered subcutaneously on a monthly basis at clinic, ensuring better adherence through professional touchpoints compared to NSAIDS or EP4s that are both daily oral regimens that owners often struggle with long-term administration. The commercial implication can be very lucrative: chronic pain management is inherently recurring, creating a durable revenue stream that will grow as pet ownership continues to increase, and pets live longer.

Clinical data shows meaningful improvement in efficacy. In dogs, a placebo-controlled study showed that day 28 success rate of 43.5% compared to 16.9% placebo, whose efficacy persisted through longer time horizon through day 56 (50.8%) and 84 (48.2%). In a head-to-head comparison against NSAID, Librela demonstrated efficacy parity with NSAID over 56 days with superior adverse event profile. There is no direct comparison against EP4s, but placebo-controlled tests indicate that Librela efficacy is on par with EP4s with better duration profile and reduced adverse events such as vomiting.

In cats, it is more difficult to run pain analysis because cats are evolutionarily wired to hide signs of discomfort; unlike dogs, they rarely vocalize or limp in a way that correlates with pain severity. Against this backdrop, Solensia demonstrated clinically meaningful improvements on validated owner and veterinarian assessed pain indices with the effect becoming more evident after two monthly injections as steady-state exposure was reached. Safety was acceptable and adherence was much better than legacy daily oral treatments.

However, recent scrutiny around adverse events have rekindled concern around class-wide NGF inhibition risks, hindering adoption momentum for Librela and Solensia. NGF plays a key role in pain transmission, but also it contributes to normal nerve health and tissue repair. This raises theoretical questions about whether long-term NGF inhibition could accelerate joint degeneration or affect other physiological processes. One report highlighted a higher incidence of adverse musculoskeletal events such as ligament /tendon injury, fractures, and polyarthritis with Librela. Although the article was criticized for potential bias and case mix, it increased clinical scrutiny. Media and SNS attention have amplified the overall concern. Therefore, real‑world pharmacovigilance and transparent communication will be critical for Zoetis to sustain confidence of owners, veterinarians, and investors.

Adoption remains early. Management cited about 1.2~1.3 million US dogs are on Librela, only a fraction of the opportunity set given its reference to 27 million OA incidents in dogs in the US and 9 million dogs being currently treated across modalities (a $2~3 billion market as of now with potential to triple if all eligible dogs were treated). Using AVMA’s 90 million total dog count in the US and 16-24% OA prevalence range, this yields an OA relevant population of 14-22 million dogs compared to the figure provided by Zoetis, reaffirming the fact that there is substantial headroom even before considering under-diagnosis and compliance.

For cats, AVMA estimates about 74 million in the US; at 40% prevalence rate, there are 29.5 million OA-relevant cats. Zoestis has not released commentary on cats, but we can assume that the adoption is in even earlier innings. With no commercial anti-NGF competition in the US, Zoetis has a huge TAM all for itself. While legacy treatments remain relevant, they are constrained by adverse events and daily dosing adherence challenges. If differentiation of monthly biologic can reset the standard of care in chronic pain management, OA pain management should provide a substantial leeway for growth and turn into a blockbuster category for Zoetis.

Rest of Companion Animal Segment Deep Dive

Zoetis’ companion animal portfolio relies less on any single headline product but more on how multiple therapies fit together to provide superior outcome. In dermatology, Apoquel, oral JAK inhibitor, has become the quick, reliable way to relieve allergic itch within 24 hours, making it the preferred choice for flares. Cytopoint, a targeted anti-IL-31 monoclonal antibody, complements fast relief by delivering 4-8 weeks of prolonged relief with benign safety profile thanks to minimal systemic immunosuppression and better adherence through vet-controlled injections.

Looking at competition, cyclosporine offers durable lesion control (albeit at a slower onset speed of 2~4 weeks) through broad immunosuppression that leads to higher risk of GI issues and infection. Oral JAK competitors such as Zenrelia have entered the market, but they are not very differentiated from Apoquel in terms of class-typical risks and lack the biologic complement that matches Cytopoint’s long-acting, targeted mechanism. Therefore, ZTS’ complementary pairing of fast relief followed by long-term control provides the strong moat for its dermatology franchise even as rivals arrive.

Parasiticides tell a similar story. For dogs, Simparica Trio (sarloner, moxidectin, and pyrantel) remains a differentiated all-in-one monthly chewable tablet solution that kills fleas fast, covers six tick species and prevents lyme infection, and added the first-in-class label to prevent flea tapeworm by killing vector fleas. Additionally, ProHeart 12 (moxidectin) is a vet-administered, annual injection where monthly adherence for the chewable solution is problematic. For cats, Revolution Plus (selamectin and sarloner) provides broad flea and tick control with heartworm prevention, ear mites, roundworms, and flea tapeworm through monthly topical spot-on solution that is applied directly to the skin at the base of the cat’s neck. Competition has intensified with Boehringer Ingelheim, Elanco, and Merk Animal Health all providing monthly combos and sometime longer dosing intervals. NexGard PLUS delivers a compelling all‑in‑one from a franchise owners already know, and Credelio Quattro adds on‑label tapeworm treatment. However, ZTS’ parasiticide portfolio still delivers the simplest, most comprehensive regimen that minimizes gaps and owner complexity and helps manage growing concerns about parasitic resistance through consistency and adherence.

Beyond the three major segments, Zoetis’ catalogue includes other pharmaceuticals, non-pharmaceuticals, diagnostics, vaccines, and anti-infectives. None of these categories are deemed to be growth drivers in the near future, but their existence helps Zoetis present itself as the one-stop-shop for clinics, strengthen relationships, and simplify purchasing decisions.

Livestock Business Overview

Once the majority of the business (65% of revenue in 2011), livestock segment is now smaller and slower growing part of the company. Zoetis has been intentionally moving away from commodity anti-infective play and into more innovation-centric vaccines to enhance growth and value-add. Recent vaccines rollout spans both species and regions – in poultry, there were approvals across Brazil, UK, and EU; in swine, a needle-free administration in Australia and EU, and in cattle, expansion of license to include use in lactating dairy cattle in the US. As anti-infective headwinds subside and vaccine programs scale across regions and species, livestock business could move beyond the cyclicality in commoditized markets and transform into a strategically differentiated asset with improved margins for Zoetis.

SOTP Valuation and DCF Walkthrough

A six-year cash flow model of companion animal business based on reasonable growth assumptions for parasiticides and dermatology (7.5% forward growth; both segments have annualized at about 17% growth during the past five years) to reflect the presence of competition and a base-case 15% growth assumption for pain and sedation to reflect low market penetration against a sizeable addressable market shows below results. For simplicity, other parts of business are assumed to grow at terminal growth rate of of 3%.

Similarly, a six-year cash flow model of livestock business based on 3% decline assumption for anti-infectives and 4% growth assumption for vaccines (both based on annualized growth rates during the past five years) shows below results. Medicated feed additives are assumed to grow at terminal growth rate of 1%.

By triangulating data from Zoetis' 2Q 2025 earnings presentation across US and international business, we can reasonably derive that companion business gross profit is about 85% while livestock is at about 45%. A sum-of-the-parts DCF valuation based on a theoretical WACC of about 7.5% (ZTS’ latest interest payments suggest about 4.3% of combined debt expense, 7.7% of cost of equity based on 0.7-0.8 beta, and about 21% tax assumption), D&A and CapEx based on historical norms, and incremental R&D on companion animals business to reflect heavier investment, leads to an EV valuation of about $79.5 billion for companion animal business, $9 billion for livestock business, netted by $17.8 billion corporate overhead adjustment.

Companion animal EV exceeds Zoetis’ consolidated SOTP, reinforcing the view that the company is fundamentally a companion animal business.
